title: JSON Duality Views
description: Bidirectional document-relational mapping. Write through documents, read through SQL, and vice versa — with full ACID guarantees.
checkpoint: Same data. Two access patterns. One source of truth. No sync. No ETL. No eventual consistency.

exercises.title: Read Through the Document Lens
exercises.description: View clients, advisors, and transactions as rich JSON documents through duality views.
exercises.explanation: This is the core of Unified Model Theory in action. The data lives in normalized relational tables — clients, accounts, holdings — but the duality view projects it as a rich nested JSON document. No ETL. No materialized copies. No sync jobs. The document is computed on read from the relational source of truth. Each view shapes the same underlying data for a different consumer: client_portfolio_dv nests accounts and holdings inside a client, advisor_book_dv shows an advisor's full book of business, transaction_feed_dv flattens a transaction with its account context. One truth, many shapes.
exercises.code:
-- View a client as a rich document (with nested accounts and holdings)
SELECT json_serialize(data PRETTY)
FROM client_portfolio_dv
WHERE data."_id" = 1001;

-- View an advisor's book
SELECT json_serialize(data PRETTY)
FROM advisor_book_dv
WHERE data."_id" = 5;

-- View a transaction with account context (unnested)
SELECT json_serialize(data PRETTY)
FROM transaction_feed_dv
WHERE data."_id" = 1;

steps.label: View client 1001 as a nested document with accounts and holdings
View advisor 5's book of business as a document
View transaction 1 with its account context

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM client_portfolio_dv WHERE json_value(data, '$._id' RETURNING NUMBER) = 1001

exercises.title: Read Through the Relational Lens
exercises.description: Access the same data relationally with SQL JOINs — same data, different access pattern.
exercises.explanation: This is the other side of the duality. The exact same data you just saw as a nested JSON document is accessible here through standard SQL JOINs across normalized tables. Client 1001's accounts and holdings — same rows, same values, different shape. The relational lens gives you the full power of SQL: GROUP BY, window functions, cross-client analytics, ad-hoc reporting. The document lens gives your application developers the shape they need without complex joins. Neither view is a copy. Neither is derived. Both are direct access to the canonical form. That's what 'duality' means.
exercises.code:
-- The SAME data, accessed relationally
SELECT c.first_name, c.last_name, c.risk_profile,
       a.account_type, a.account_name,
       h.symbol, h.quantity, h.market_value
FROM clients c
JOIN accounts a ON a.client_id = c.client_id
JOIN holdings h ON h.account_id = a.account_id
WHERE c.client_id = 1001
ORDER BY a.account_type, h.market_value DESC;
exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM clients c JOIN accounts a ON a.client_id = c.client_id JOIN holdings h ON h.account_id = a.account_id WHERE c.client_id = 1001

exercises.title: Write Through the Document API
exercises.description: Insert a new client with accounts and holdings through the duality view — one document insert populates three relational tables.
exercises.explanation: Here's where duality views go from interesting to transformative. You INSERT a single JSON document — a client with nested accounts and holdings — and Oracle decomposes it into the correct rows across three normalized tables: clients, accounts, and holdings. Foreign keys, primary keys, referential integrity — all handled automatically. The duality view definition tells Oracle how to map document fields to relational columns, and the database does the decomposition transactionally. One INSERT, one COMMIT, three tables populated, full ACID. Try doing that with a document database and a relational database sitting side by side.

steps.label: Insert a new client document with nested accounts and holdings
Verify the relational tables were populated automatically

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM clients WHERE email = 'sarah.chen@example.com'

exercises.title: Update Through the Document API
exercises.description: Update a holding's market value through the duality view and verify the relational table reflects the change.
exercises.explanation: JSON_TRANSFORM works on duality views the same way it works on raw JSON columns — but the magic is what happens underneath. When you SET a path inside the document, Oracle traces that path back to the specific relational column it maps to and issues the corresponding UPDATE on the underlying table. The holdings table's market_value column gets updated directly. No application logic. No ORM. No read-modify-write cycle. The duality view is bidirectional: read as documents, write as documents, and the relational truth stays consistent. Verify it yourself — query the holdings table directly.

steps.label: Update the market value of the first holding through the duality view
Verify the relational holdings table reflects the change
steps.code:
SELECT h.symbol, h.market_value
FROM holdings h
JOIN accounts a ON h.account_id = a.account_id
JOIN clients c ON a.client_id = c.client_id
WHERE c.email = 'sarah.chen@example.com';

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT h.market_value AS mv FROM holdings h JOIN accounts a ON h.account_id = a.account_id JOIN clients c ON a.client_id = c.client_id WHERE c.email = 'sarah.chen@example.com' AND h.symbol = 'NVDA'

exercises.title: Delete Through the Document API
exercises.description: Delete a client through the duality view and verify the cascade cleanup.
exercises.explanation: DELETE on a duality view cascades through the relational structure based on the view's definition. When you delete the client document, Oracle removes the client row, and the cascade directives (defined by the WITH DELETE clause in the duality view DDL) clean up the associated accounts and holdings rows automatically. This is referential integrity doing what it was designed to do — the database enforces the cleanup, not your application code. The verification query confirms zero rows remain. No orphaned records. No dangling references. The relational model's constraints work for you, even when you're operating through a document API.

steps.label: Delete the client document through the duality view
Verify the client and related data were removed from relational tables
steps.code: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM clients WHERE email = 'sarah.chen@example.com';

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM clients WHERE email = 'sarah.chen@example.com'

exercises.title: Examine the Duality View Definition
exercises.description: Inspect the metadata that describes how the duality view maps between document and relational shapes.
exercises.explanation: Oracle exposes the full duality view mapping through three system views. USER_JSON_DUALITY_VIEW_TABS shows which relational tables back the view — this tells you where your document's data actually lives. USER_JSON_DUALITY_VIEW_TAB_COLS shows the column-level mapping: which JSON field maps to which relational column, and the data type conversions involved. USER_JSON_DUALITY_VIEW_LINKS shows the relationships between tables — the foreign key paths Oracle traverses to assemble the nested document. This is full transparency into the shape projection. You can see exactly how canonical form becomes projected shape.

steps.label: List the relational tables backing the duality view
Show column-level mappings between JSON fields and relational columns
Show the foreign key relationships used to assemble the document

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM user_json_duality_view_tabs WHERE view_name = 'CLIENT_PORTFOLIO_DV'

exercises.title: Create Your Own Duality View
exercises.description: Challenge: Create a holdings summary duality view that shows each holding with its account and client context using UNNEST.
exercises.explanation: Now you're building a duality view from scratch. UNNEST is the key operator here — it flattens a nested subquery's fields into the parent document instead of creating an array. Without UNNEST, the account and client data would appear as nested objects; with it, accountName and clientName appear as top-level fields alongside the holding data. WITH NOCHECK marks the accounts and clients tables as read-only in this view — you can read through them but writes only target the holdings table (marked WITH UPDATE). This is deliberate: the view is shaped for a holdings-centric consumer who needs context but only modifies holding values. Model the domain. Project the access.
exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM user_json_duality_views WHERE view_name = 'MY_HOLDINGS_DV'
